body:
## Summary

When running `$ --isolated docker -- <command>`, the spawned container's lifecycle is not tied to "the work is done." Depending on mode it is either always removed (attached) or never removed by default (detached). Neither default matches the intuitive contract:

> **Run the command in a throwaway container, and once the command finishes, remove the container so no CPU, RAM, or disk is wasted after completion — while always preserving the full log that `$` already collects.**

This issue proposes making **"remove the container filesystem when the command finishes" the default for `$ --isolated docker`**, with the log kept regardless, plus an explicit opt-out to keep the filesystem for investigation.

## Current behavior (as of `main`)

Two code paths in `runInDocker()` (`js/src/lib/isolation.js`), selected by `getEffectiveMode()` (docker defaults to **attached**, `js/src/lib/args-parser.js:915-921`):

**Attached mode (the default), `js/src/lib/isolation.js:708`:**
```js
const dockerArgs = ['run', '-it', '--rm', '--name', containerName];
```
- `--rm` is hardcoded → container auto-removed on exit. Good for disk, BUT:
- No file log is written on this path — it runs with `stdio: 'inherit'` (`isolation.js:743`); output only streams to the terminal. So the "throwaway container" default sacrifices the persisted full log.

**Detached mode (`--detached`), `js/src/lib/isolation.js:616-623`:**
```js
const dockerArgs = ['run', '-d'];
if (options.keepAlive) { dockerArgs.push('-i', '-t'); }
dockerArgs.push('--name', containerName);
if (options.autoRemoveDockerContainer) {
  dockerArgs.push('--rm');
}
```
- `--rm` only if the user passes `--auto-remove-docker-container` (default `false`, `args-parser.js:184`).
- So **by default the detached container is left as an exited container, filesystem preserved indefinitely** (`isolation.js:691-695` even prints "Container filesystem will be preserved after exit.").
- There is **no** subsequent `docker stop`/`docker rm` anywhere; the only removal mechanism is the daemon-side `--rm`.

**Cleanup never depends on exit status.** Attached always removes; detached removes only per-flag — neither path looks at whether the command succeeded or failed (`isolation.js:758` captures `success` only for reporting).

### Why this matters

Downstream consumers run `$ --isolated docker --detached` for long tasks (e.g. `link-assistant/hive-mind`'s isolated `gh-issue-solver` runs). Because detached preserves by default, **every finished task leaves a multi-GB writable layer** (`target/debug`, `node_modules/onnxruntime`+CUDA, browser binaries…). On one server these accumulated to **~35 GB across a handful of finished tasks**. Nothing reaps them.

## Proposed behavior

1. **Default = remove container filesystem when the command finishes**, for both attached and detached docker isolation. The container is a throwaway sandbox; once the command exits, its CPU/RAM/disk should be reclaimed automatically.

2. **Always keep the full log**, by default, regardless of removal. The log `$` already collects (`js/src/lib/isolation-log-utils.js` → `.../start-command/logs/isolation/docker/<id>.log`) lives on the **host**, not inside the container, so removing the container fs does not lose it. Two correctness fixes this requires:
   - **Attached path must also write the file log** (today it only inherits stdio and writes no file — `isolation.js:743`). Tee it to the same log path the detached path uses, so the "remove by default" contract still yields a persisted log.
   - **Detached removal must not race the log/exit-code capture.** The detached log + exit code are read via `docker logs -f <name>` and `docker inspect -f '{{.State.ExitCode}}' <name>` (`isolation.js:671-682`). A plain `--rm` can delete the container the instant the command finishes, truncating the log and losing the exit code. So removal must happen **after** `docker logs -f` drains and the exit code is captured — i.e. an explicit post-completion `docker rm <name>` rather than relying solely on daemon `--rm`.

3. **Add an explicit opt-out to keep the filesystem for investigation**, e.g. `--keep-container` (and/or `--keep-container-on-failure`). When set, the container is left in place after the command finishes, and `$` **prints the exact cleanup command** the user can run later, e.g.:
   ```
   Container kept for investigation: <containerName>
   Inspect: docker exec -it <containerName> sh    (or: docker start -ai <containerName>)
   Remove when done: docker rm -f <containerName>
   ```

4. **(Optional, recommended) success-vs-failure-aware default:** remove on success, keep on failure. A failed run is exactly when you want the filesystem to investigate; a successful one has nothing worth keeping. This pairs naturally with #3 — on a kept (failed) container, print the cleanup command. `$` already knows the exit code at this point (`isolation.js:758`).

### Summary of the desired default for `$ --isolated docker`
- command finishes successfully → **remove container fs**, keep log, free all resources.
- command fails → **keep container fs** for investigation, keep log, print the `docker rm -f <name>` cleanup command.
- `--keep-container` → always keep + print cleanup command.
- `--auto-remove-docker-container` (existing) → always remove even on failure.

## Relevant code (for whoever implements)

- `js/src/lib/isolation.js`
  - `runInDocker()` — lines 561–781
  - attached `docker run -it --rm` (unconditional) — line 708; `stdio: 'inherit'`, no file log — line 743; exit handling / `success` — lines 741–763
  - detached `docker run -d` + conditional `--rm` — lines 616–623
  - detached log streaming (`docker logs -f` + `docker inspect` exit code) — lines 671–682
  - "Container filesystem will be preserved after exit." message — lines 691–695
- `js/src/lib/args-parser.js`
  - docker defaults to attached (`getEffectiveMode`) — lines 915–921
  - `autoRemoveDockerContainer` default `false` — line 184; parse — lines 446–449
- `js/src/lib/isolation-log-utils.js`
  - `createLogPath()` — lines 1378–1389
- Rust mirror: `rust/src/lib/isolation.rs`, `rust/src/lib/isolation_log.rs`, `rust/src/lib/args_parser.rs` — same change needed for parity.

## Related

- Companion issue in `link-assistant/hive-mind` (the main downstream consumer): it runs `$ --isolated docker --detached --session <uuid>` and relies on this default; finished task containers there accumulate multi-GB layers. It can also implement success/failure-aware removal on its side since it independently detects the terminal exit code.

comments.body: Downstream companion issue filed: link-assistant/hive-mind#1979 — Hive Mind runs `$ --isolated docker --detached --session <uuid>` per task, hits the detached path that preserves the container by default, and never reaps it; finished task containers accumulate multi-GB writable layers (~35 GB observed on one host). It can implement success/failure-aware removal on its own exit-status detection, but making removal the default here (with the log kept) is the upstream fix.

We should have `--keep-container`, and `--always-cleanup-container` - the default, and also `--keep-container-on-fail` which should be the same as `--auto-remove-docker-container`.

So by default we should cleanup at the moment, later we may reconsider, but all configuration options should be available now, so later it will be easy to switch.

title: $ --isolated docker: remove container filesystem when the command finishes (keep the log), with opt-out to keep it for investigation
